title: President's Lecture Series: Dr. Robert Langer
text:
Good morning everyone. Can you hear me?
Good morning and it's a pleasure to
welcome you to the president's lecture
series here in the beautiful Brockman
Hall for Opera on an equally beautiful
day here on Rice's campus. Over the
years, this series has brought a variety
of stimulating speakers to the Rice
stage. Our last speaker, which actually
was several years ago, Eric Juan with
the founder and CEO of Zoom, spoke via
Zoom during the CO 19 pandemic about the
evolution of the virtual platform. Rice
alumni Dr. Barney Graham and Bill Gruber
gave a lecture about their personal
involvement in the creation of the CO 19
vaccine. and several Nobel Prize
winners, authors, business owners, and
pioneering leaders in higher education
have given keynote addresses on a wide
range of topics. Their knowledge and
experiences have enhanced the um
intellectual life of the Rice community
and of our neighbors throughout the city
of Houston.
Today, we are honored to host Dr. Robert
Langanger, a renowned scientist,
engineer, and professor at the
Massachusetts MI Institute of
Technology, better known as MIT. He is
often called the Edison of medicine. Dr.
Langanger is one of the most cited
engineers in history and has made
pioneering contributions to drug
delivery, systems, and tissue
engineering, revolutionizing modern
medicine. He has mentored thousands of
uh undergraduates, graduate students,
postocs who are in their own right stars
around the country. Several of them here
at Rice University. A graduate of
Cornell University with a bachelor's
degree in chemical engineering. Dr.
Langanger went on to earn his doctor of
science in chemical engineering from MIT
where he holds the title of institute
professor, the highest honor awarded to
an MIT faculty member.
Over the course of his extraordinary
career, Dr. Langanger has received more
than 220 major awards. So, let me repeat
that. He has received more than 220
major awards. It's not a typo, and I
promise not to mention all of them,
including the United States National
Medal of Science and the National Medal
of Technology and Innovation, two of the
highest honors bestowed by the US
government. He is also a recipient of
the Queen Elizabeth Prize for
Engineering, the Breakthrough Prize in
Life Sciences, and the Charles Stark
Draper Prize, and most recently the 2023
Priestly Medal, the highest honor of the
American Chemical Society.
He is one of only a few individuals ever
elected to all threemies, the National
Academy of Sciences, the National
Academy of Engineering, and the National
Academy of Medicine. A testament to the
breath and impact of his work. It's just
a coincidence that Rice has had his its
first trifecta this past week when
Professor Lydia Kvaki was elected to
National SC National Academy of Science,
giving her that same great honor, our
first in Rice's history. Dr. Langanger's
work exemplifies the profound impact
that interdisiplinary research can have
on society. His innovations have not
only advanced scientific understanding,
but have also led to practical
applications that improves lives. His
co-founding of Mona, the company behind
the first CO 19 vaccines, demonstrates
how scientific discoveries can translate
can be translated into lifesaving
therapies.
As we continue to navigate the
challenges of the modern world, the
importance of such translational
research cannot be overstated. It is
through the dedication of individuals
like Dr. Langanger that we find
solutions to some of the most pressing
issues of our time. Please join me in a
warm Texas welcome for Dr. Robert
Langanger.
[Applause]
[Music]
Thank you so much. It's a tremendous
honor to be here today and to see all of
you. Um so I'd like to tell you a little
bit about uh my career and what we've
tried to do. So the lecture I'm going to
give is a little bit different than a
lot of them I do which are purely
academic but because what I'm also going
to try to go over is how we took those
academic inventions or discoveries and
work with others to create companies to
create products like you just heard in
that in introduction. Um, so
I I actually uh entitled my uh talk The
Road Not Taken, which is named from
Robert Frost poem, because as you'll
see, I didn't really do exactly what a
lot of other other chemical engineers
did. But I I thought I'd make a summary
slide of of part of what I was going to
discuss, which is innovation.
and and and this is sometimes what I
tell my own students is to do things
that you hope will have a very big
impact as opposed to incremental
research. That might seem like a very
straightforward piece of advice, but it
isn't. You know what if people often go
into academics, you know, the way they
get judged might be their grants or
their papers. And if you do something
incremental, you can certainly get a
paper and those papers will help you get
grants.
Same thing in com companies. If you go
out to work in a company, a lot of
people, I mean, there's nothing wrong
with this is you want a secure life and
and so it's much easier to do, you know,
something where you know you're going to
get paid and you're not going to change
the world, but I think that if you want
a certain degree of satisfaction, you
know, maybe it's taking risks. Second
thing I was going to say is conventional
wisdom. What you hear and sometimes read
in the textbooks, it's not always
correct and challenging that can be
important.
The third is something I call
convergence. Uh at least for me is the
idea of being exposed to very different
disciplines. I think it's something
we're even seeing a little bit in
academia now in the sense that for years
like when I was in college there was a
building for English, a building for
math, building for chemistry. Now
sometimes people get put together and
they learn things from students and
professors who have very different
backgrounds from their own. The fourth
is to take risks and realizing that it's
okay to fail. I think that's one of the
great things about American culture. You
know, I I I'm not a fan of Hershey. I
love chocolate as some of my students
know. I'm not necessarily a fan of
Hershey's chocolate, but I love the
story of Milton Hershey who had this
dream very different than my own, but
his dream was to start a candy store and
he tried about eight times and he failed
each of those times, but not the ninth.
Fifth is if your idea is important,
expect lots of criticism. And six, I I
used to say never give up, but now I say
never give up almost. So with that, let
me tell you a little bit about how I got
started and and and and my struggles and
my dreams. So the way I got started, I
got my degree uh from MIT in chemical
engineering in 1974.
And that year there was this gas
shortage and um and what happened if you
lived in Boston area where I did uh you
had to wait in line for two hours to get
your tank filled up. But the consequence
of that is that if you were a chemical
engineer, you got a lot of job offers.
So pretty much all my friends, they went
to work for oil companies. I thought I'd
do that, too. So I interviewed I
actually got 20 job offers, four from
Exxon alone
including Exxon. I think there was one
in Baytown and one anyhow but um so so
and and believe me I wasn't that good
but they just had so many openings. But
you know when I I I I when I flew back
to Boston I think about what I was
sometimes asked to do which was
increasing the yield of certain
chemicals by small percentages. And I
just thought to myself, I I didn't want
to spend my life doing that. I didn't
know exactly what I did want to do, but
I thought, I want to do something that
would help people. And when I was a
graduate student at MIT, and I was
supposed to be doing my thesis, I got
very and I I did some, but I but what I
did is I I helped uh start a school for
uh workingclass and low-income high
school students. Um and I got very
involved in creating new chemistry
curricula and new math curriculum. And
one day I saw an ad from City College of
New York to do just that. So I wrote
them a letter applying for a job, but
they didn't write me back. But I really
like that idea. I found all the ads I
could to do something like uh create new
chemistry curriculum as an assistant
professor. I wrote found about 40 of
them and I wrote them all and and I
don't think any wrote me back. So that
wasn't so good. So I started to think
what other way could I use my chemical
engineering background to help people?
And I thought about medicine. So, I
wrote to a lot of hospitals and medical
schools. They they didn't write me back
either. One day, I remember walking into
the lab where I was at MIT and one of
the older scientists said to me, he
said, "Bob," he says, "there's a surgeon
in Boston named Judah Folkman." And he
said, "Sometimes he hires unusual
people."
He he thought very highly of Dr.
Folkman. I won't say what he thought
about me, but but Dr. Folkman had this
idea that tumors, this was from the this
actually was from the New York Times in
the early 1970s, but he had an idea
which at that time was very
controversial, but basically saying that
tumors grow because they get blood
vessels and they even secrete chemicals
which he called tumor angioenesis factor
that cause the surrounding blood vessels
to actually grow when they're normally
don't at all. But they grow and they
grow and they feed the tumor and the
tumor gets bigger and bigger. And if you
go to the, you know, top of that slide,
you can see how vascularized it is
diagrammatically. And of course, what
often kills people is spreading through
those blood vessels and setting up in
other places. That's a process called
metastasis.
So it turns out, as I mentioned, that
many biologists uh didn't believe this.
I I since I knew almost no biology, I
thought it sounded quite good. Um, but
any rate, I he hired me. He said, 'Well,
Bob, you your job is to prove I'm right
and in so doing, could you isolate the
very first substances that could stop
blood vessels from growing? Now, the
thing is is if you work in an area like
this, like finding a new factor, the
biggest problem, if you look back on
this historically, is having what we
call a bioassay to study that. And there
wasn't one to study blood vessels. And
it's not easy because wherever you go in
the body, usually they're background
blood vessels, but there's a few places
in organisms and people um where there
are not. One is uh the the cornea and
two is what we thought is the what's
called the chick coral and membrane.
That's from a chicken. So we had an idea
that we would create a bioassay by
putting certain tumors for the biologist
that was a V2 carcinoma uh in in in
let's say the cornea and that would
cause blood vessels but what we would
want next to it is
uh is is basically uh a little um
delivery system a nano particle or
microparticle that could deliver any
molecule we want to isolate and Dr.
Fopman's theory and was that the
molecules we wanted to deliver were very
large like proteins or polysaccharides
or nucleic acids. Now this was 1974. In
1974 the area of advanced drug delivery
systems was almost non-existent. There
was only one company in the world a
company called Alza in Palo Alto
California working on this. They would
later and no products but they would
later commercialize what are called
transdermal patches and they had Nobel
Prize winners on their advisory board uh
in chemistry and so Dr. Fman went out
there and he asked them could they help
us
but they all said well no you know maybe
you could deliver a small molecule but
you'd never be able to deliver a large
molecule because they would be too big
to pass through you know the walls of a
tiny particle just like none of us will
be able to walk through that wall. But
if we were going to solve the blood
vessel problem, we had to solve this
delivery problem. So I spent several
years working on this and I actually
found over 200 different ways to get
this to not work.
But eventually I we were able to make
these tiny particles. You see a picture
of one and one cut in half and uh and
and I'm sorry. Um
yeah, you see a picture of one and one
cut in half. And um what we were able to
show and we published this in the
journal nature is that if you um
put different large molecules in them,
you can deliver them. This was this was
49 years ago and in fact this is the
very first paper that shows that you can
actually create tiny particles to
deliver nucleic acids. For reference,
DNA and RNA are nucleic acids. So I
remember uh this you know I I got asked
in 1976.
This was uh three months before this
paper came out to give a a talk for the
major talk for the first time in my
life. Uh I was 27 years old. I practiced
this talk for many weeks. Finally the
day came. It was in Midland Michigan. I
got up and I gave the talk and it was
like I say 20 minutes and I thought at
the end of it I I did okay. I didn't
forget too much of what I was going to
say. I didn't stammer too much. So, I
thought when I was done, all these older
scientists being uh nice people.
I I can tell it's the scientists who are
laughing uh but uh because they know.
And and and I thought they'd want to
encourage me, this young guy. But when I
got done, I stepped off the podium and a
whole bunch of them came up to me and
they said, "We don't believe anything
you just said."
They said that the molecules again uh
were too big and the solvents that we
were using like ethanol or methylene
chloride, they destroy whatever we put
in. And things actually went downhill
from there. I I tried to get research
grants, you know, you write your grants
to like National Institutes of Health
and places like that and they have study
sections and they review them. My first
nine were rejected and they weren't just
rejected. They were pretty um firm about
how bad I was. One of them said, "I
remember Dr. Langanger is an engineer.
He knows nothing about biology and even
less about oncology."
I thought I knew enough about math to
know that was pretty hard to do, but it
didn't really matter. Um so I didn't do
very well on grants. And then, you know,
I love being a posttock at at Boston
Children's Hospital and and and Harvard
Medical School, medical school. I was
actually the only engineer there. I
learned so much. But a lot of my friends
would tell me, you know, maybe I should,
you know, get a real job someday. And so
I did. I started applying to uh for
faculty positions in 1977. And I had
applied uh to chemical engineering
departments because that's my
background. And no chemical engineering
department uh in the country would offer
me a job. They actually said the
opposite of the study section. and they
said well you want to do this bio stuff
you should be doing you know um energy
or or mathematical modeling or you know
stuff like that and anyhow so I didn't
get any jobs there finally I got a job
actually in a nutrition department and
the reason I think I got it is uh the
guy who was the head of that department
Nevin Scrimshaw very famous nutritionist
but he was what I'd call a benevolent
dictator kind of department head meaning
he liked me so he offered me a job but
he didn't ask anybody body else in that
department any of the professors what
they thought.
Now that might have been okay except for
one thing which was the year after I
joined the department he left.
So a lot of the senior faculty decided
to give me advice and their advice is I
ought to leave too
and this is true you know one of my good
friends uh at the time and still is Mike
Marleta. Mike later would become head of
Berkeley's chemistry department, the
head of scripts. And about 15 years ago,
I got an award and they asked if he'd
say a few words. So I uh I excerpted
this from his talk. He was referring to
1979. And what he says is one evening he
went to a faculty dinner at a Chinese
restaurant with me and some senior MIT
professors. He said, "A senior scientist
set quizzing us while smoking a cigar."
He said, "When the elder scientist heard
my concepts for drug delivery, he blew a
cloud of smoke in my face and said, "You
better start looking for another job."
Fortunately, I'm I'm still at MIT. But
it was a kind of not easy beginning. But
since we had developed the drug delivery
systems, now we could do the bioassay
that I mentioned. So, I'm going to show
you a fairly graphic picture. There's no
other way to show blood vessels, but so
if anybody doesn't like blood, don't
look. But but what you're going to see
is from a paper in science we wrote in
1976 which does show for the first time
that diffusible substances can stop
blood vessels from growing. So I will
show it rather quickly.
And here you see on the left the sheet
what this is what you see in a control
we did probably 2,000 of those because I
had almost every fraction we tested
didn't work. But on the right you can
see that you can get inhibition and that
would show do both those things that he
wanted. It shows that those molecules
would exist and it would provide a
pathway for all future um inhibitors to
to to be found. Now medicine as as a lot
of you may know it doesn't move fast. We
published that paper in 1976. As I
mentioned, it took 28 years and
literally great work from companies like
Genitech and spending billions of
dollars be in using a lot of the
approaches we developed, but it took
them 28 years before the first blood
vessel inhibitor would get approved. But
starting in 2004, and this may be hard
to see in the back, it doesn't really
matter. The point I want to make is
there are a lot of them now. But
starting in 2004, a vast gets approved.
A vast is the number two or three
bestselling biotech tech in history.
Even last year, 20 years after its
approval, was the number four
bestselling drug in the world. And you
can see on this slide that there are
many many blood vessel inhibitors
approved for literally all kinds of
cancer. And not only do these are are
these end up being used for all kinds of
cancer, they are the only
pharmacological entities that can be
used to prevent diseases of blindness in
the back of your eye. Diseases like
macular degeneration which is the
leading cause of blindness in our
country or diabetic retinopathy. All
these drugs have become, you know,
blockbusters, multibillion dollar a year
drugs. And Nature, the journal, uh,
estimates it'll be used by half a
billion people worldwide.
I want to go back though to the 1970s.
Um, not not this, you can see this keeps
going on to today. Going back to the
1970s, you know, Dr. Fman and I talked
about, well, maybe we should write write
a patent. You know, now I I have quite a
few patents, but then I had zero. and
Children's Hospital which in Boston
which is a very good hospital we zero
there too. So we wrote a patent five
years in a row it got rejected. So I
remember Al Brazagini the head of the
tech transfer office at that hospital
coming to see me one day he said Bob you
you're wasting so much money for the
hospital on these patents they're never
going to allow it. He said just quit but
but you can that was one of my things on
the first slide I I don't like to quit.
So, but I started to think how could we
convince the patent examiner to allow
our patent legally of course
and and and science really wasn't
working. But when I mentioned this to
you in the beginning, I told you that
all these people we talked to said this
was impossible. It could never work. I
wondered maybe somebody wrote that down.
So, what I did in 1982 is do what's
called the science citation search.
meaning I looked back to see who cited
our 1976 nature paper and what they said
and I found some interesting papers. I
will just show you one and this is from
five of the top material scientists in
the world. I had no idea it was written.
I'll just read it to you. They're
describing this field and they said
generally the agent to be released is a
relatively small molecule with a
molecular weight no larger than a few
hundred. One would not expect that
macroolelecules could be released
because of their extremely small
permeation rates. However, Folkman and
myself have reported some surprising
results that clearly demonstrate the
opposite. So, I really like that word
surprising. And uh I showed it to Dr.
Brazzagini. He flew it out to Washington
to show it to the patent examiner. And
the patent examiner said, "Gee, I had he
had no idea." He said, "I tell you what,
I will allow this patent if Dr.
Langanger can get written affidavits
from each of these five people that they
really wrote that
it it it's a true story." And by the
way, if you ever want to get patents and
you get your grants rejected, you save
the quotes. At any rate, uh, so I did I
got they're all I was assistant
professor. I wrote them and they were
all nice enough to write me back. They
really did write it. We got this really
broad patent. So now I'd been working on
this for about 10 years. You know, like
I said, I had this dream. I wanted
something that I did to make a
difference. and fi and I finally got
calls first from one multi-billion
dollar company in animal health and then
just a bit later a second one in human
health and they said you know we we read
about your work would you consult for us
would you we're going to we'd like to
give you a big grant and we're going to
develop this and I I was so happy about
that but you know what happened is that
um I did I did get a better car I had a
Ford Pinto at the time but at any rate I
I you know what happened is each of them
worked on it for about a year and then
they gave up. They didn't get exactly
the results they wanted. So, Alex
Cleonoff, one of my good friends, he
said, "Bob, we should start our own
company." So, with four of my students,
we started this little company and it I
got the patents back. Uh I started we
started this company. We did this merger
with our next door neighbor Alchemies,
which is and the company is still around
today. They have 25 products that are
FDA approved or in clinical trials. New
treatments for schizophrenia,
alcoholism, opioid addiction, type 2
diabetes. just to show you some pictures
um of of some of these products and
other things that came from our lab
either because of our patents or because
of uh you know helping companies using
principles we developed. I I made this
slide and you can see uh I mean there
are many different things on here.
Lupron Depot in the middle we worked
with Teada that's a a treatment for
advanced prostate cancer that that's
delivering a peptide that's nine amino
acids. If you try to swallow it or take
it nasally, you just don't get any in
the blood. If you inject it, it's
destroyed almost immediately. So now
they put it in little microspheres.
Originally they lasted a month. Now they
last six months and it's one of the
world's largest major ways of treating
advanced prostate cancer and
endometriosis. But there are many many
other ones up here. Vivitrol is one of
the most common treatments for opioid
addiction. Uh and there's other
treatments for schizophrenia and and up
here and so forth. Not only are they
useful in medical things on the bottom
right um you there they turn out Yanni
Zohar who is a one of the world's
experts in aquaculture came to visit me
and what we did is we started making uh
little implants that are used to
preserve uh control spawning and
actually even used to preserve rare fish
and that would end up helping make uh
aquaculture a quarter of a trillion
dollar industry. So because I had
started uh the a company um and and and
we were doing this kind of work in
chemical engineering which was now much
more experimental than just theoretical
you know we'd get I'd get more and more
students and postocs coming to me. So
I'll tell you a second story. This is
David Edwards. David Edwards uh actually
when he came to me uh to our lab he had
written two books with Howard Brener and
he had published 30 papers. He had never
done an experiment in his life. But what
he did was a lot of mathematical
modeling. One of the things he modeled
was the lung. So when he came to our
lab,
he um you know, I told him I thought we
could really do something in the aerosol
area. Aerosols,
which would be another drug delivery
vehicle, are incredibly inefficient.
You're lucky if you take an inhaler and
you get 2% from the device into the
lung. You might ask why. Well, if you
take People always thought aerosols had
to be really tiny. They thought if they
were big that they'd settled in the back
of your throat, but if they're tiny,
it's kind of like wet sand, right?
There's so much surface area, they'll
stick together. They'll stick together
uh in in the device, in the air, in your
mouth. So, very little gets to the lung.
So, how did people try to change that?
Well, they designed better and better
devices to break apart those sandlike
aggregates. and they, you know,
aerosolizers and and that would enable
them to go from 2% maybe to 3%. But
which is still not much. The problem
with getting so low efficiency is not
just the cost, it's also that for many
drugs you'd have to have a giant
inhaler.
So, um, anyhow, nobody ever really tried
to change the aerosol. I mean, everybody
kept trying to change the inhaler, the
device. So what David and I started
talking about was well every at this
time this was now the early 1990s almost
all the inhalers were but meter dose
inhalers or nebulizers there you're you
know nebulizing basically water there
were a few what are called dry powder
inhalers but the density of whether
you're dealing with water or the dry
powders in that case they were always
one roughly roughly one gram per cc and
we'd call that a non-porous aerosol and
so what David started doing is
mathematical modeling. So, what if we
made these aerosols really big but
incredibly porous, they will not stick
in the back of their throat. They will
end up going into the deep lung. There's
something called an aerodynamic
aerodynamic diameter. So, so what we
decided to do is make large, you know,
aerosols but incredibly porous ones. And
the hope would be that you'd get they
wouldn't stick together like right wet
basketballs aren't going to stick
together like wet sand. There's just
much less surface area. Also, we thought
they might last longer because
macrofasages when you get in the lung
there cells that like to eat these
aerosols. It's kind of like rather than
eating a small meal which doesn't take
you long. Now what the cell sees is this
big meal. So that might take longer.
These were our theories. So, we made
some of these uh and I'll just show you
a picture and I'll give you a sports
analogy. So, before we were involved, if
you ever looked at an aerosol, it might
look like a little golf ball or a little
baseball. Now, they look like whiffle
balls. It's it's really that simple. But
that change in geometry changes
everything. If you go to an invitro
experiment, what you see is you can
actually on the right hand side get 10
times more in. If you go to an invivo
experiment using what's called forced
inhilation, you can again get about 10
times more in. And if you do uh this is
published in science. If you do a human
experiment, you can actually make these
last for three days. So again, we filed
the patent. I won't keep showing you all
our patents, but um but basically this
patent is is one of the things we kept
doing because it was important to have
broad patents to raise the kind of
funding we needed to get these to
people. I I should add when we tried to
write our grants on this to the National
Institutes of Health and other places,
they were soundly rejected. People said
they this wouldn't work. Um I got used
to that. Uh but uh at any rate, um just
to show you what the claim is, it's a
particulate system and the particles
have a density less than 4 g per cc.
Normally they're about 0.1 gram per cc,
but they're much bigger than ever
before. And notice that the claim is for
any agent. So we started this company,
we called it air uh in 1996.
We published the paper in science in 97.
We started air with a whole bunch of our
students uh 1998 and Terry Maguire at
Polaris uh gave us a quarter of a
million dollars to start it and did the
business development work where we'd
make deals with Lily, Glaco, Visor and
actually started two clinical trials. It
was easier to start the trials because
all we did was change the geometry. We
didn't change the chemistry and you know
about four companies wanted to buy it
and David didn't who was really running
it. He he didn't want to run it forever.
So we did sell it and I this is one of
the highest returns I guess in the
history of of biotech. We also were able
to, you know, a lot of people always say
that patents are some people say patents
are bad, but if we didn't have the
patent, we would never have made the
progress that we made because we
wouldn't have had the funding. And David
actually spent off a non a nonprofit uh
to to make these aerosols for the
developing world. Anyhow uh and and the
cop the technology kept going through
different uh you know places and
ultimately gets FDA approved now and the
principles of course widely used but I
just want to show you this is actually
approved it's ambregia it and and this
is the little device that you can have
